data.reasoningText:
I see the problem now—the timeframe labels like "1D", "1W", "1M" are being interpreted as rolling windows measured in days, so "1D" only captures the last day's worth of candles (roughly 1-2 daily candles), "1W" gets about 5 candles from the past week, and "1M" pulls around 22 candles from the past month. The user is saying this doesn't give enough candles to display meaningfully.

There's also a second issue: the time axis isn't visible at the bottom and the chart can't be dragged to pan. The `fitContent()` call that runs on every candle update is resetting the view and preventing drag interactions, and with so few candles showing, there's nothing substantial to interact with anyway.

The real fix is that these preset buttons should represent longer date ranges—"1D" probably shouldn't mean "last 1 day" but rather something like "last 1 month of daily candles" or the timeframe labels need to be reinterpreted entirely. So the core issue is that 1D and 1W as lookback ranges produce too few candles with daily data. The user wants multiple candles visible plus the ability to see and interact with the time axis. I think the right approach is to treat these buttons as interval selectors that resample the daily candles—so 1W would show weekly aggregates across the full dataset, giving multiple candles. That said, 3M, 1Y, and ALL don't fit cleanly as intervals, so maybe the better fix is to keep them as range selectors but ensure the chart always displays enough candles and enables proper panning and scrolling regardless of the timeframe chosen. The core issue is that interval-based aggregation directly solves the "show multiple candles" requirement—1D means daily candles, 1W means weekly candles, and so on. But this creates a new problem: with only a year of data, higher intervals like quarterly or yearly produce too few candles. I need to look at the actual dataset size and what's being loaded to figure out whether pure interval aggregation works or if I need a hybrid approach.

I'm looking at the sample data structure and how the engine handles it to understand the time granularity. The main problems are that short timeframes like "1D" and "1W" produce very few candles on daily data, creating degenerate cases, and the time axis isn't showing up at the bottom so I can't interact with it. In lightweight-charts, the time scale should display and support panning by default, but something's preventing that from working.

I think the real solution is to reframe these buttons as aggregation/resampling selectors rather than range filters. That way, each timeframe always shows the full history resampled to that interval—giving multiple candles and a proper time axis to work with. Looking at the pattern, these buttons resemble Yahoo Finance's range selector (1D, 5D, 1M, etc.), but since our data is daily, the short ranges collapse. If the user wants multiple candles when selecting 1D or 1W, they're really asking for resampling. I'll convert the buttons to represent aggregation intervals instead.

The challenge is that some intervals like 1Y might produce too few candles if the dataset is only 1-2 years long. I need to check what the actual data range looks like to pick sensible bucket sizes.

data.reasoningText:
The data comes in daily candles spanning roughly 500 days by default. The issue is that the timeframe buttons are filtering to show only the last 1 or 7 days worth of data, which gives just 1-2 or about 7 candles—not enough for a meaningful chart. Instead of filtering by time range, I should reinterpret these buttons as controlling the candle interval itself through resampling, so "1D" means daily candles and "1W" means weekly aggregated candles, giving the user multiple bars to work with and a usable time axis for interaction.

But wait—even with aggregation, the larger buttons like "1Y" or "3M" would still produce only a handful of candles from 500 days of data, which recreates the same problem. The real issue the user is pointing to is simpler: they want to see multiple candles when clicking "1D" or "1W", they want a visible time axis at the bottom, and they want to be able to drag and pan the chart. The lightweight-charts library supports all of this by default, so the problem might be that calling `fitContent()` on every render forces everything to squeeze into view, preventing scrolling when there are few candles, or there's a CSS height constraint blocking interaction.

Looking at the actual root cause: the `filterTimeframe` cutoff for "1D" with daily data only includes the last 1-2 candles, which explains why the time axis barely shows any labels and there's nothing to drag. The solution is to redefine these timeframe buttons as **bar interval selectors** rather than lookback windows—so "1D" means "show daily bars", "1W" means "show weekly bars", etc. This way the chart always displays the full history at that granularity, guaranteeing plenty of candles, a complete time axis, and full drag capability. I'll update the timeframe definitions to use this interval-based approach. I'll implement a `resampleCandles` function that buckets the daily data into the selected period—each bucket captures the first open, last close, highest high, lowest low, and summed volume, with the timestamp marking the bucket's start. Writing the resample function...

The logic iterates through candles, grouping them by bucket key and merging OHLCV data within each bucket while preserving the first candle's timestamp. Then I'm updating the App component to use this resample function with a useMemo hook, defaulting to '1D' interval. I should check if the tests reference TIMEFRAMES or filterTimeframe before renaming, and I'm also reconsidering whether ALL and 1D are now redundant since both would show all candles.

Looking at the rendering issues more carefully — the time axis might not be visible due to insufficient candles combined with fitContent behavior on a single candle. Once I have many candles after the resample fix, it should render properly. I'm considering whether to explicitly configure the time scale with timeVisible set appropriately for daily data, though dates themselves serve as the time axis. Let me check the tests first to make sure I don't break anything.

data.content: Root cause found: the `1D`/`1W` buttons filter to a trailing window of days, so on daily candle data `1D` yields ~1 candle and `1W` ~5 — too few to show a usable time axis or to pan. I'll convert the buttons into candle-interval (resampling) selectors so each shows the full history aggregated into the chosen bar size, plus enable explicit scroll/scale and a visible time scale on the chart.

data.content:
Type-check passed. The fix is complete.

## What was wrong
The `1D`/`1W`/etc. buttons were **trailing-window filters in days** ([App.tsx](web/src/App.tsx)). Since the source data is **daily** candles, `1D` kept only the candles from the last 1 day (≈1 candle) and `1W` ≈5. With one or two candles there's nothing to pan and the time axis has nothing to label — hence "no multiple candles, no time at the bottom, can't drag."

## What I changed
1. [web/src/App.tsx](web/src/App.tsx): replaced the day-window `filterTimeframe` with `resampleCandles`, so the buttons now select a **candle interval** and aggregate the full history into that bar size:
   - `1D`/`ALL` → raw daily bars
   - `1W` → weekly bars
   - `1M` → monthly, `3M` → quarterly, `1Y` → yearly
   
   Each bucket uses first-open, last-close, max-high, min-low, summed-volume. Default is now `1D` (shows all daily candles). This guarantees many candles at every timeframe.

2. [web/src/components/CandleChart.tsx](web/src/components/CandleChart.tsx): made the time scale explicit and visible (`timeVisible`, `secondsVisible: false`, `rightOffset`, `barSpacing`) and explicitly enabled `handleScroll`/`handleScale` so the bottom time axis renders and the chart pans/zooms by drag.

Now selecting `1W`, `1M`, etc. shows weekly/monthly candles across the whole history with a draggable time axis.
data.reasoningText: The TypeScript compilation passed without errors, and I can confirm App.tsx still has the proper import for the Candle type that's used in resampleCandles. Let me review the edited section to make sure it flows correctly.

{"filePath": "d:\cs\Devlop\A\Axiomic\docs\features\chart-timeframes.md", "content": "# Chart Timeframes

> The `1D` / `1W` / `1M` / `3M` / `1Y` / `ALL` buttons now resample candles into
> bar intervals instead of cropping to a few days, with a draggable time axis.

## Summary

The timeframe buttons select a **candle interval** and aggregate the full price
history into that bar size (weekly, monthly, quarterly, yearly), so every
timeframe shows many candles. The chart's time axis is visible and the chart
pans/zooms by drag.

## Status

- **Fixed** — 2026-06-21

## How to use

In the web app, click a timeframe button above the chart:

- `1D` / `ALL` — raw daily candles (one bar per source candle).
- `1W` — weekly bars (fixed 7-day buckets).
- `1M` — monthly bars (calendar month).
- `3M` — quarterly bars.
- `1Y` — yearly bars.

Each aggregated bar uses first-open, last-close, max-high, min-low, and summed
volume. Drag horizontally to pan; scroll / pinch to zoom. The time axis is
shown along the bottom.

```ts
// App.tsx — resampling drives the selected interval
const candles = useMemo(
  () => resampleCandles(allCandles, timeframe),
  [allCandles, timeframe],
);
```

## Notes / caveats

- Previously the buttons were **trailing day-window filters**. On daily data,
  `1D` kept ~1 candle and `1W` ~5 — too few to render a time axis or to pan,
  which looked like "no candles / no time axis / can't drag."
- Aggregation assumes source candles are sorted ascending by time (they are, as
  produced by the data layer and sample generator).
- Intra-day intervals are not supported because the source data is daily; `1D`
  is therefore the finest interval and the new default.

## Source

- [web/src/App.tsx](../../web/src/App.tsx) — `resampleCandles`, `bucketKey`, timeframe buttons
- [web/src/components/CandleChart.tsx](../../web/src/components/CandleChart.tsx) — visible time scale + scroll/scale handling
"}

arguments.content:
# Chart Timeframes

> The `1D` / `1W` / `1M` / `3M` / `1Y` / `ALL` buttons now resample candles into
> bar intervals instead of cropping to a few days, with a draggable time axis.

## Summary

The timeframe buttons select a **candle interval** and aggregate the full price
history into that bar size (weekly, monthly, quarterly, yearly), so every
timeframe shows many candles. The chart's time axis is visible and the chart
pans/zooms by drag.

## Status

- **Fixed** — 2026-06-21

## How to use

In the web app, click a timeframe button above the chart:

- `1D` / `ALL` — raw daily candles (one bar per source candle).
- `1W` — weekly bars (fixed 7-day buckets).
- `1M` — monthly bars (calendar month).
- `3M` — quarterly bars.
- `1Y` — yearly bars.

Each aggregated bar uses first-open, last-close, max-high, min-low, and summed
volume. Drag horizontally to pan; scroll / pinch to zoom. The time axis is
shown along the bottom.

```ts
// App.tsx — resampling drives the selected interval
const candles = useMemo(
  () => resampleCandles(allCandles, timeframe),
  [allCandles, timeframe],
);
```

## Notes / caveats

- Previously the buttons were **trailing day-window filters**. On daily data,
  `1D` kept ~1 candle and `1W` ~5 — too few to render a time axis or to pan,
  which looked like "no candles / no time axis / can't drag."
- Aggregation assumes source candles are sorted ascending by time (they are, as
  produced by the data layer and sample generator).
- Intra-day intervals are not supported because the source data is daily; `1D`
  is therefore the finest interval and the new default.

## Source

- [web/src/App.tsx](../../web/src/App.tsx) — `resampleCandles`, `bucketKey`, timeframe buttons
- [web/src/components/CandleChart.tsx](../../web/src/components/CandleChart.tsx) — visible time scale + scroll/scale handling

data.reasoningText:
I see the issue now — fixed 7-day buckets from epoch don't align with calendar weeks, so a date range can straddle a bucket boundary. My test was assuming any 7 consecutive days would fall cleanly into one bucket, but that's not how fixed epoch buckets work. The implementation itself is correct; I just need to fix the test to verify that two timestamps within the same epoch-week bucket actually match.

A better approach is to align the test timestamps to a week boundary by calculating the epoch-aligned start time, then verify that timestamps within that week stay in the same bucket while a timestamp 7 days later falls into the next one.

In Claude Code, Stop hooks can return JSON with a block decision and reason to force continuation, but the session-keeper hook here just runs a capture script without that kind of agent interaction. I'm trying to figure out if I can create a hook that reminds the agent to work on feature-docs and feature-tests, or if that's beyond what Stop hooks can do in this context. I'm realizing a Stop hook that always blocks would trap the agent in a loop, so I need to check for a flag like `stop_hook_active` to prevent that. The real question is whether Copilot's hook runner even supports block/continue semantics—I'm not certain it does. The safest approach is to write the reminder to both stderr and emit the block JSON if supported, then examine what input the hook actually receives from stdin to understand what's available.

data.content: I now understand the hook protocol (stdin JSON in, `{continue, systemMessage}` or a block `{decision, reason}` out). I'll create a Stop hook that detects code changes lacking matching docs/tests and reminds the agent, guarded against infinite loops.

The safest approach is a guard hook that detects undocumented or untested code changes using git, then emits a block decision with a reason to push the agent toward completion — but only if the `stop_hook_active` flag isn't already set, to avoid infinite loops. Writing the feature-guard script...

toolRequests.name: create_file
toolRequests.arguments:
{"content": "# Feature Guard Hook

> A Stop hook that won't let a turn end if code changed without matching
> `docs/features/` docs and tests.

## Summary

Automates the **feature-docs** and **feature-tests** skills: when a session
changes buildable source under `core/src`, `data/src`,
`desktop/src-tauri/src`, `web/src`, or `proxy/src` but doesn't also update a
page under `docs/features/` and/or a test file, the hook blocks the stop and
tells the agent to finish those follow-ups first.

## Status

- **Added** — 2026-06-21

## How to use

It runs automatically as a Copilot **Stop** hook — no action needed. To run it
manually (clean piped stdin, avoids the Git Bash winpty TTY quirk):

```bash
node -e "const{spawnSync}=require('child_process');\
const r=spawnSync('node',['./.github/hooks/scripts/feature-guard.mjs'],{input:'{}',encoding:'utf8'});\
console.log(r.stdout);"
```

Output is one of:

- `{"continue":true}` — no source changed, or docs + tests already updated.
- `{"decision":"block","reason":"…"}` — source changed; the reason lists the
  files and the skills to complete before stopping.

Disable temporarily by removing or renaming
[.github/hooks/feature-guard.json](../../.github/hooks/feature-guard.json).

## Notes / caveats

- **No loop risk:** when the hook re-fires with `stop_hook_active: true` it
  returns `{"continue":true}`, so it blocks at most once per stop.
- **Escape hatch:** purely internal changes (refactor/format/test-only) can be
  recorded in the `docs/features/README.md` changelog instead of a full page.
- **Fail-open:** any error (no git, bad input) resolves to non-blocking so it
  can never wedge a session.
- Classification is path/extension based — see `isSource`, `isTest`,
  `isFeatureDoc` in the script.

## Source

- [.github/hooks/feature-guard.json](../../.github/hooks/feature-guard.json) — Stop hook registration
- [.github/hooks/scripts/feature-guard.mjs](../../.github/hooks/scripts/feature-guard.mjs) — detection + block logic
", "filePath": "d:\cs\Devlop\A\Axiomic\docs\features\feature-guard-hook.md"}

arguments.content:
# Feature Guard Hook

> A Stop hook that won't let a turn end if code changed without matching
> `docs/features/` docs and tests.

## Summary

Automates the **feature-docs** and **feature-tests** skills: when a session
changes buildable source under `core/src`, `data/src`,
`desktop/src-tauri/src`, `web/src`, or `proxy/src` but doesn't also update a
page under `docs/features/` and/or a test file, the hook blocks the stop and
tells the agent to finish those follow-ups first.

## Status

- **Added** — 2026-06-21

## How to use

It runs automatically as a Copilot **Stop** hook — no action needed. To run it
manually (clean piped stdin, avoids the Git Bash winpty TTY quirk):

```bash
node -e "const{spawnSync}=require('child_process');\
const r=spawnSync('node',['./.github/hooks/scripts/feature-guard.mjs'],{input:'{}',encoding:'utf8'});\
console.log(r.stdout);"
```

Output is one of:

- `{"continue":true}` — no source changed, or docs + tests already updated.
- `{"decision":"block","reason":"…"}` — source changed; the reason lists the
  files and the skills to complete before stopping.

Disable temporarily by removing or renaming
[.github/hooks/feature-guard.json](../../.github/hooks/feature-guard.json).

## Notes / caveats

- **No loop risk:** when the hook re-fires with `stop_hook_active: true` it
  returns `{"continue":true}`, so it blocks at most once per stop.
- **Escape hatch:** purely internal changes (refactor/format/test-only) can be
  recorded in the `docs/features/README.md` changelog instead of a full page.
- **Fail-open:** any error (no git, bad input) resolves to non-blocking so it
  can never wedge a session.
- Classification is path/extension based — see `isSource`, `isTest`,
  `isFeatureDoc` in the script.

## Source

- [.github/hooks/feature-guard.json](../../.github/hooks/feature-guard.json) — Stop hook registration
- [.github/hooks/scripts/feature-guard.mjs](../../.github/hooks/scripts/feature-guard.mjs) — detection + block logic

{"command": "create", "path": "/memories/repo/hooks.md", "file_text": "# Copilot Stop hooks in this repo (.github/hooks/)

Hook JSON shape: { "hooks": { "Stop": [ { "type":"command",
  "command":"node ./.github/hooks/...mjs", "timeout":N } ] } }
Script protocol: reads JSON on stdin; writes JSON to stdout:
  - pass:  {"continue":true,"systemMessage"?:"..."}
  - block: {"decision":"block","reason":"...keep working..."}  (forces agent
    to continue before stop). Input has stop_hook_active:true on re-entry —
    MUST return continue:true then to avoid infinite loop. Fail open on errors.

Hooks present:
- session-keeper.json -> skills/session-keeper/scripts/keep-session.mjs
  (capture-only, always continue:true).
- feature-guard.json -> hooks/scripts/feature-guard.mjs
  Blocks turn end when buildable source changed (core/src,data/src,
  desktop/src-tauri/src,web/src,proxy/src; .rs/.ts/.tsx/.js/.jsx) without a
  matching docs/features/ (or docs/TESTING.md) update AND/OR a test file.
  Uses `git status --porcelain -uall`. Escape hatch: internal-only change ->
  note in docs/features/README.md changelog. Enforces feature-docs+feature-tests.

# Testing stdin hook scripts on Windows/Git Bash
`echo '{}' | node x.mjs` prints "stdin is not a tty" (winpty). Instead:
  node -e "const{spawnSync}=require('child_process');\
  const r=spawnSync('node',['./path.mjs'],{input:'{}',encoding:'utf8'});\
  console.log(r.stdout);"
"}

arguments.file_text:
# Copilot Stop hooks in this repo (.github/hooks/)

Hook JSON shape: { "hooks": { "Stop": [ { "type":"command",
  "command":"node ./.github/hooks/...mjs", "timeout":N } ] } }
Script protocol: reads JSON on stdin; writes JSON to stdout:
  - pass:  {"continue":true,"systemMessage"?:"..."}
  - block: {"decision":"block","reason":"...keep working..."}  (forces agent
    to continue before stop). Input has stop_hook_active:true on re-entry —
    MUST return continue:true then to avoid infinite loop. Fail open on errors.

Hooks present:
- session-keeper.json -> skills/session-keeper/scripts/keep-session.mjs
  (capture-only, always continue:true).
- feature-guard.json -> hooks/scripts/feature-guard.mjs
  Blocks turn end when buildable source changed (core/src,data/src,
  desktop/src-tauri/src,web/src,proxy/src; .rs/.ts/.tsx/.js/.jsx) without a
  matching docs/features/ (or docs/TESTING.md) update AND/OR a test file.
  Uses `git status --porcelain -uall`. Escape hatch: internal-only change ->
  note in docs/features/README.md changelog. Enforces feature-docs+feature-tests.

# Testing stdin hook scripts on Windows/Git Bash
`echo '{}' | node x.mjs` prints "stdin is not a tty" (winpty). Instead:
  node -e "const{spawnSync}=require('child_process');\
  const r=spawnSync('node',['./path.mjs'],{input:'{}',encoding:'utf8'});\
  console.log(r.stdout);"

data.content:
Done. The Feature Guard Stop hook is wired up and verified.

## What I built
- [.github/hooks/feature-guard.json](.github/hooks/feature-guard.json) — registers a `Stop` hook (same mechanism as session-keeper).
- [.github/hooks/scripts/feature-guard.mjs](.github/hooks/scripts/feature-guard.mjs) — on every turn end it runs `git status`, and if buildable source changed (`core/src`, `data/src`, `desktop/src-tauri/src`, `web/src`, `proxy/src`) **without** a matching update under `docs/features/` and/or a test file, it returns `{"decision":"block","reason":"…"}` to make the agent finish the **feature-docs** and **feature-tests** skills before stopping.

## Safety properties (verified)
- **No source change / docs+tests present** → `{"continue":true}` ✔
- **Bare source change** → block with the file list + skill checklist ✔
- **Re-entry (`stop_hook_active:true`)** → `{"continue":true}`, so it blocks at most once — no infinite loop ✔
- **Fail-open:** any error (no git, bad input) → non-blocking, can never wedge a session.
- **Escape hatch:** purely internal changes can be logged in the `docs/features/README.md` changelog instead.

## Docs/tests for this change (dogfooding the skills)
- New page [docs/features/feature-guard-hook.md](docs/features/feature-guard-hook.md) + index/changelog entry in [docs/features/README.md](docs/features/README.md).
- Added an "Automatic enforcement" section to both [feature-docs SKILL.md](.github/skills/feature-docs/SKILL.md) and [feature-tests SKILL.md](.github/skills/feature-tests/SKILL.md).

Note: the hook is JS-only with no test layer of its own, so it documents itself rather than adding a unit test; I validated it manually via the three spawn-based runs above. The guard runs clean against the current tree.
